Participant_7_CaseStudy-29.1-GEMINI.fail_reason: Does not create clear action hierarchy - all actions still feel similar.

Participant_7_CaseStudy-29.1-OPENAI.pass_reason: Preserved the original modal layout and improved action hierarchy.
Participant_7_CaseStudy-29.1-OPENAI.fail_reason: “Rate Us” still competes with the main exit/cancel decision.

Participant_7_CaseStudy-30.1-OPENAI.pass_reason: Correctly repositions the “in-store mode” floating action button to avoid overlapping the bottom navigation while maintaining overall layout consistency.
Participant_7_CaseStudy-30.1-OPENAI.fail_reason: The button spacing from the bottom edge is slightly excessive, making it feel visually detached from the navigation area.

Participant_7_CaseStudy-31.1-CLAUDE.pass_reason: Integrated the “Accuracy” accordion item cleanly while maintaining consistent divider lines, typography, and spacing.
Participant_7_CaseStudy-31.1-CLAUDE.fail_reason: The accordion rows appear marginally taller than the original, making the list feel slightly less compact.

Participant_7_CaseStudy-31.1-GEMINI.pass_reason: Successfully added the extra FAQ item without breaking the existing styling or accordion layout.
Participant_7_CaseStudy-31.1-GEMINI.fail_reason: The bottom section feels slightly more compressed vertically, reducing breathing room above the feedback button.

Participant_7_CaseStudy-31.1-OPENAI.pass_reason: Added the new “Accuracy” FAQ item while preserving the original accordion structure, spacing, and visual hierarchy.
Participant_7_CaseStudy-31.1-OPENAI.fail_reason: The added FAQ item slightly tightens the spacing near the feedback button at the bottom.

Participant_7_CaseStudy-32.1-CLAUDE.pass_reason: Added clear visual emphasis to the subscription actions while keeping the modal highly consistent with the original interface, typography, and spacing system.
Participant_7_CaseStudy-32.1-CLAUDE.fail_reason: The secondary “Cancel” action became too visually muted relative to the rest of the dialog, reducing discoverability and making the action hierarchy slightly unbalanced.

Participant_7_CaseStudy-32.1-GEMINI.pass_reason: Improved the visibility and affordance of the billing action buttons while maintaining consistency with the app’s existing Material-style UI and preserving the dialog structure.
Participant_7_CaseStudy-32.1-GEMINI.fail_reason: The dialog spacing and button sizing feel slightly heavier than the original design, making the modal appear denser and less balanced compared to the surrounding lightweight interface.

Participant_7_CaseStudy-32.1-OPENAI.pass_reason: Clearly improved the purchase dialog’s action hierarchy by turning “Monthly” and “Yearly” into filled high-contrast buttons, making the primary subscription actions much easier to identify while preserving the original layout and spacing.
Participant_7_CaseStudy-32.1-OPENAI.fail_reason: The filled buttons are visually stronger, but the equal emphasis between “Monthly” and “Yearly” misses an opportunity to guide users toward the recommended yearly plan with a slightly more prominent treatment or savings highlight.

Participant_7_CaseStudy-33.1-CLAUDE.pass_reason: Improved CTA prioritization while maintaining strong consistency with the existing UI style, especially through the warm golden highlight that matches the app’s premium visual tone. The updated hierarchy feels intentional and conversion-focused without disrupting surrounding sections.
Participant_7_CaseStudy-33.1-CLAUDE.fail_reason: The glow/shadow effect around the yearly CTA adds slightly more visual weight than necessary and pulls attention away from nearby content.

Participant_7_CaseStudy-33.1-GEMINI.pass_reason: The yearly subscription option is emphasized more clearly than before and the overall layout remains mostly intact.
Participant_7_CaseStudy-33.1-GEMINI.fail_reason: The new bright pink CTA introduces a stronger visual shift that feels less aligned with the existing premium black-and-gold visual language of the interface, making the screen feel slightly less cohesive compared to the original design system.

Participant_7_CaseStudy-33.1-OPENAI.pass_reason: Successfully improved the visual hierarchy by making the “Yearly Subscribe” CTA significantly more prominent while still preserving the original structure, spacing, and premium dark aesthetic of the paywall. The stronger contrast clearly guides user attention toward the intended subscription option without introducing layout inconsistencies.
Participant_7_CaseStudy-33.1-OPENAI.fail_reason: The highlighted yearly CTA becomes visually dominant enough that the “Life-time Purchase” option now feels slightly de-emphasized compared to the original balance.

Participant_7_CaseStudy-34.1-CLAUDE.pass_reason: Improved the visual hierarchy effectively by increasing the prominence of the CTA while maintaining strong consistency with the original UI style, typography, and premium dark theme. The pricing cards and CTA feel visually cohesive and conversion-focused.
Participant_7_CaseStudy-34.1-CLAUDE.fail_reason: The CTA still competes slightly too aggressively with the pricing cards due to the strong brightness contrast, making the lower section feel heavier than the rest of the layout.

Participant_7_CaseStudy-34.1-GEMINI.pass_reason: The updated CTA is clearer and more noticeable than the original while keeping most of the existing layout intact. The screen still feels functional and readable overall.
Participant_7_CaseStudy-34.1-GEMINI.fail_reason: The button styling and emphasis feel less refined and slightly disconnected from the premium visual language of the rest of the paywall, making the hierarchy feel less balanced compared to the original design.

Participant_7_CaseStudy-34.1-OPENAI.pass_reason: Successfully improved the CTA hierarchy by making the “Subscribe Now” button more visually prominent while preserving the original dark premium aesthetic and spacing consistency of the paywall. The stronger contrast helps guide users toward the final conversion action without disrupting the overall structure.
Participant_7_CaseStudy-34.1-OPENAI.fail_reason: The glow effect around the CTA is slightly too intense and pulls attention away from the Annual and Quarterly pricing cards, which should remain the primary comparison area before conversion.
